name: N

unions.name: BelowOrAbove
unions.comment:
The error result of comparing [`N`](#N)s

  - `Above`: greater than what it's compared against
  - `Below`: less than what it's compared against

Values exist for each condition

unions.name: FixedValue
unions.comment:
In some cases, it's better or required not to store functions etc. in app state, events, ...:

  - serializability, for example
      - json import/export on the debugger ‚Üí doesn't work
  - calling `==`, for example
      - on hot module reloading,
        old code might end up remaining in the model
      - [lamdera](https://www.lamdera.com/) ‚Üí doesn't work
      - accidental `==` call ‚Üí crash

Calling `==` on a [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue) will yield the correct result instead of crashing

[`Fixed` is defined as a difference from 0](#Fixed), so this independent type needed to be created

You can just use [`Fixed`](#Fixed) when you don't have disadvantages storing functions

unions.name: In
unions.comment:
somewhere within a minimum & maximum

       ‚Üì minimum   ‚Üì maximum
    ‚®Ø [‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì] ‚®Ø ‚®Ø ‚®Ø...


### argument type in a range

    -- 3 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 5
    N (In (Fixed (Add3 minX_)) (Up maxTo5_ N5))

    -- 0 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 100
    percent : N (In min_ (Up maxTo100_ To N100)) -> Percent

For every constructable value, the minimum is smaller than the maximum

If you want a number where you just care about the minimum, leave `max` as a type _variable_

       ‚Üì minimum    ‚Üì maximum or  ‚Üí
    ‚®Ø [‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì...

    -- any natural number
    N (In min_ max_)

A number, at least 5:

    N (In (Add5 minMinus5_) max_)

‚Üí `max_` could be a specific maximum or [no maximum at all](#Infinity)


### result type in a range

    n3 : N (In (Up3 minX_) (Up3 maxX_))

    between3And6 : N (In (Up3 minX_) (Up6 maxX_))

    between3And6 |> N.add n3
    --: N (In (Up6 minX_) (Up9 maxX_))


### stored type in a range

An example where this is useful using [typesafe-array](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/lue-bird/elm-typesafe-array/latest/):

    type Tree branchingFactor element
        = Tree
            element
            (ArraySized
                branchingFactor
                (Maybe (Tree branchingFactor element))
            )

    type alias TreeBinaryFull element =
        Tree (Exactly N2) element

Remember: ‚Üë and other [`Min`](#Min) `(` [`Fixed`](#Fixed) `...)` / [`Exactly`](#Exactly) / [`InFixed`](#InFixed) aren't argument types

---

Do not use `==` on 2 values storing a range.
It will lead to elm crashing because [difference](#Up)s are stored as functions.
Instead,

  - [compare](#compare) in _your_ code
  - convert to [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue) for _other_ code
    that relies (or performs better) on structural `==`

unions.name: N
unions.comment:
A **bounded** natural number `>= 0`


### result type

    -- ‚â• 4
    N (Min (Up4 x))

    -- 2 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 12
    N (In (Up2 minX_)) (Up12 maxX_))

    -- n3 :
    N (In (Up3 minX_) (Up3 maxX_))

This enables adding, subtracting.
Consider the "Up" thing an implementation detail

    n3
        |> N.add n6
        --: N (In (Up9 minX_) (Up9 maxX_))
        |> N.toValue
    --> n9 |> N.toValue


### argument type

    -- ‚â• 0, any limitations allowed
    N range_

    -- ‚â• 4
    N (In (Fixed (Add4 minMinus4_)) max_)

    -- 4 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 15
    N (In (Fixed (Add4 minMinus4_)) (Up maxTo15_ To N15))

`In (Add4 minMinus4_) (Up maxTo15_ To N15)` says:

  - the minimum-constraint can be `4+ 0`|`4+ 1`|`4+ 2`|...
    which means it's ‚â• 4
  - the argument's maximum `+` some variable `maxTo15` is `15`
    which means it's ‚â§ 15


### stored type

what to put in declared types like `Model`

    -- ‚â• 4
    N (Min (Fixed N4))

    -- 2 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 12
    N (InFixed N2 N12)

    -- = 3
    N (Exactly N3)

There's also versions of this that don't contain functions internally:

    -- ‚â• 4
    N (Min (FixedValue N4))

    -- 2 ‚â§ n ‚â§ 12
    N (InFixedValue N2 N12)

    -- = 3
    N (ExactlyValue N3)

more type examples at [`In`](#In), [`Min`](#Min)

unions.name: N0OrAdd1
unions.comment:
Base type of [`N0`](#N0), [`Add1 n`](#Add1) following [`allowable-state`](https://dark.elm.dmy.fr/packages/lue-bird/elm-allowable-state/latest/):

  - Is the parameter `possiblyOrNever` set to `Never` like in

        type alias Add1 n =
            N0aOrAdd1 Never n

    `N0` is impossible to construct

  - Is the parameter `possiblyOrNever` set to `Possibly` like in

        type alias N0 =
            N0OrAdd1 Possibly Never

    `N0` is a possible value

unions.name: To
unions.comment:
Just a word in a [difference type](#Up):

    Up low To high

    Down high To high

‚Üí distance `high - low`

unions.name: Up
unions.comment: `Up low To high`: an exact number as the difference `high - low`

aliases.name: Down
aliases.comment: `Down high To low`: an exact number as the difference `high - low`
aliases.type: N.Up low toTag high

aliases.name: Exactly
aliases.comment:
Allow only a specific [`Fixed`](#Fixed) number

Useful as a **stored & argument** type
in combination with [`typesafe-array`](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/lue-bird/elm-typesafe-array/latest/)s,
not with [`N`](#N)s

    byte : ArraySized (Exactly N8) Bit -> Byte

‚Üí A given [`ArraySized`](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/lue-bird/elm-typesafe-array/latest/) must have _exactly 8_ `Bit`s

    type alias TicTacToeBoard =
        ArraySized
            (Exactly N3)
            (ArraySized (Exactly N3) TicTacToField)

‚Üí A given [`ArraySized`](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/lue-bird/elm-typesafe-array/latest/) must have _exactly 3 by 3_ `TicTacToeField`s
aliases.type: N.InFixed n n

aliases.name: ExactlyValue
aliases.comment:
For **storing** in a type. Allow only a specific [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)

You can just use [`Exactly`](#Exactly) when you don't have disadvantages storing functions.
See [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)
aliases.type: N.InFixedValue n n

aliases.name: Fixed
aliases.comment:
The [difference](#Up) from [`0`](#N0) to a given `n`

A stored type looks like a [result type](#result-type)
where every [`Up<n> x`](#Up) is instead [`Fixed N<n>`](#Fixed)

Do not use `==` on 2 numbers in a [`Fixed` range](#InFixed).
It will lead to elm crashing because [difference](#Up)s are stored as functions internally.
Instead,

  - [compare](#compare) in _your_ code
  - convert to [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue) for _other_ code
    that relies (or performs better) on structural `==`
aliases.type: N.Up N.N0 N.To n

aliases.name: InFixed
aliases.comment:
Lower and upper limits [`Fixed`](#Fixed). For stored types only

Can't store functions? ‚Üí [`InFixedValue`](#InFixedValue)
aliases.type: N.In (N.Fixed min) (N.Fixed max)

aliases.name: InFixedValue
aliases.comment:
Lower and upper limits as [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)s. For stored types only

You can just use [`InFixed`](#InFixed) when you don't have disadvantages storing functions.
See [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)
aliases.type: N.In (N.FixedValue min) (N.FixedValue max)

aliases.name: Infinity
aliases.comment:
Flag "The number's upper limit is unknown" used in the definition of [`Min`](#Min):

    type alias Min min =
        In minimum Infinity
aliases.type: N.Fixed { infinity : () }

aliases.name: InfinityValue
aliases.comment:
[`Infinity`](#Infinity) as a [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue). Used in

    type alias MinValue min =
        In (FixedValue min) InfinityValue

You can just use [`Infinity`](#Infinity) when you don't have disadvantages storing functions.
See [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)
aliases.type: N.FixedValue { infinity : () }

aliases.name: Min
aliases.comment:
Only **stored / result types should use the type `Min`**:

       ‚Üì minimum    ‚Üì or ‚Üí
    ‚®Ø [‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì ‚úì...


### result type without maximum constraint

Sometimes, you simply cannot compute a maximum

    absoluteInt : Int -> N (In (Up0 x_) ??)
                    ‚Üì
    absoluteInt : Int -> N (Min (Up0 x_))

    -- n ‚â• 5
    atLeast5 : N (Min (Up5 x_))

    atLeast5 |> N.addMin n3
    --: N (Min (Up8 x_))

    n3 |> N.addMin atLeast5
    --: N (Min (Up8 x_))


### argument type without maximum constraint

Every `Min min` is of type `In min ...`,
so using a type variable for the maximum on arguments is highly encouraged
when no maximum constraint should be enforced

    -- any natural number
    N (In min_ max_)

    -- number, at least 5
    N (In (Add5 minMinus5_) max_)


### stored type without maximum constraint

An example where this is useful using [typesafe-array](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/lue-bird/elm-typesafe-array/latest/):

    type Tree branchingFactor element
        = Tree
            element
            (ArraySized
                branchingFactor
                (Maybe (Tree branchingFactor element))
            )

    type alias TreeMulti element =
        Tree (Min (Fixed N1)) element

Remember: ‚Üë and other [`Min`](#Min)/[`Exactly`](#Exactly)/[`Fixed`](#Fixed) are result/stored types, not argument types

Can't store functions? ‚Üí [`MinValue`](#MinValue)
aliases.type: N.In lowestPossibleValue N.Infinity

aliases.name: MinValue
aliases.comment:
A lower limit as a [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue). For stored types only

You can just use [`Min`](#Min) `(` [`Fixed`](#Fixed) `...)` when you don't have disadvantages storing functions.
See [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)
aliases.type: N.In (N.FixedValue min) N.InfinityValue

values.name: absoluteInt
values.comment:
The `Int`'s distance from `0`.
This ["absolute value"](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Absolute_value) is always `‚â• 0`

    -4
        |> N.absoluteInt
        --: N (Min (Up0 x_))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 4

    16 |> N.absoluteInt |> N.toInt
    --> 16

Really only use this if you want the absolute value

    badLength =
        List.length >> N.absoluteInt

  - maybe, there's a solution that never even theoretically deals with unexpected values:

        mostCorrectLength =
            List.foldl
                (\_ -> N.addMin n1 >> N.minDown n1)
                (n0 |> N.maxToInfinity)

  - other times, though, like with `Array.length`, which isn't `O(n)`,
    you can escape with for example

        arrayLength =
            Array.length >> N.atLeastInt n0
values.type: Basics.Int -> N.N (N.Min (N.Up0 x_))

values.name: add
values.comment:
Add a given [specific](#In) [`N`](#N)

    between70And100 |> N.add n7
    --: N (In (Up77 minX_) (Up107 maxX_))

One addend has an unconstrained maximum? ‚Üí [`addMin`](#addMin)
values.type: N.N (N.In (N.Up minPlusX N.To sumMinPlusX) (N.Up maxPlusX N.To sumMaxPlusX)) -> N.N (N.In (N.Up minX N.To minPlusX) (N.Up maxX N.To maxPlusX)) -> N.N (N.In (N.Up minX N.To sumMinPlusX) (N.Up maxX N.To sumMaxPlusX))

values.name: addDifference
values.comment:
Increase a [natural number](#N0OrAdd1),
like shown in a given [difference](#Up)
values.type: N.Up low N.To high -> low -> high

values.name: addMin
values.comment:
To the [`N`](#N) without a known maximum-constraint,
add a number that (only) has [information on how to add](#Up) the minima

    atLeast70 |> N.addMin n7
    --: N (Min (Up77 x_))

    n7 |> N.addMin atLeast70
    --: N (Min (Up77 x_))

Use [`add`](#add) if both maxima are known [difference](#Up)s as well

If the added minimum is [`Fixed`](#Fixed), supply the [`N.minTo`](#minTo) manually
to re-enable adding both minimum types!

    atLeast5 |> N.addMin (atLeastFixed2 |> N.min n2)
    --: N (Min (Up7 x_))
values.type: N.N (N.In (N.Up minPlusX N.To sumMinPlusX) addedMax_) -> N.N (N.In (N.Up minX N.To minPlusX) max_) -> N.N (N.Min (N.Up minX N.To sumMinPlusX))

values.name: atLeast
values.comment:
**Cap** the [`N`](#N) to `>=` a given new lower limit

    between5And12 |> N.atLeast n10
    --: N (In (Up10 x_) (Fixed N12))

The type doesn't forbid that the lower limit you're comparing against
is below the current lower limit

    n15
        |> N.atLeast n10
        --: N (In (Up10 x_) (Fixed N15))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 15

Don't know both maxima? ‚Üí [`atLeastMin`](#atLeastMin)
values.type: N.N (N.In minNew (N.Up minNewMaxToMax_ N.To max)) -> N.N (N.In min_ (N.Fixed max)) -> N.N (N.In minNew (N.Fixed max))

values.name: atLeastInt
values.comment:
A `N (Min ...)` from an `Int`;
if the `Int < minimum`, `minimum` is returned

    0
        |> N.atLeastInt n3
        --: N (Min (Up3 x_))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 3

    9
        |> N.atLeastInt n3
        --: N (Min (Up3 x_))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 9

You can also use this as an escape hatch
if you know an `Int` must be at least `minimum`.
But avoid it if you can do better, like

    goodLength =
        List.foldl
            (\_ -> N.addMin n1 >> N.minDown n1)
            (n0 |> N.maxToInfinity)

To handle the case `< minimum` yourself ‚Üí [`isAtLeastInt`](#isAtLeastInt)
values.type: N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin lowerLimitMax_) -> Basics.Int -> N.N (N.Min lowerLimitMin)

values.name: atLeastMin
values.comment:
**Cap** the [`N`](#N) to `>=` a given new lower limit

    n5AtLeast |> N.atLeastMin n10
    --: N (Min (Up10 x_))

The type doesn't forbid that the lower limit you're comparing against
is below the current lower limit

    n15AtLeast |> N.atLeastMin n10 |> N.toInt
    --: N (Min (Up10 x_))

Know both maxima? ‚Üí [`atLeast`](#atLeast)
values.type: N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin lowerLimitMax_) -> N.N range_ -> N.N (N.Min lowerLimitMin)

values.name: atMost
values.comment:
**Cap** the [`N`](#N) to `<=` a given new upper limit

    atLeast3
        |> N.atMost (between4And5 |> N.min n3)
    --: N (In (Up3 minX_) (Up5 maxX_))

  - To enforce a new minimum, too ‚Üí [`in_`](#in_)
values.type: N.N (N.In min upperLimitMax) -> N.N (N.In min max_) -> N.N (N.In min upperLimitMax)

values.name: differenceAdd
values.comment: Chain the [difference](#Up) [`Up`](#Up) to a higher [natural number](#N0OrAdd1)
values.type: N.Up middle N.To high -> N.Up low N.To middle -> N.Up low N.To high

values.name: differenceInfinity
values.comment: [Difference up](#Up) to [`Infinity`](#Infinity)
values.type: N.Infinity

values.name: differenceSubtract
values.comment: Chain the [difference](#Up) [`Down`](#Down) to a lower [natural number](#N0OrAdd1)
values.type: N.Down high N.To middle -> N.Up low N.To high -> N.Up low N.To middle

values.name: differenceToInt
values.comment:
A [difference](#Up) represented as an `Int` `>= 0`

    N.inInt ( n3, n6 ) 5
        |> N.max
        |> N.differenceToInt
    --> 6

This enables for example [`exactly`](#exactly)
values.type: N.Up low_ N.To high_ -> Basics.Int

values.name: divideBy
values.comment:
Divide (`//`) by an [`N`](#N) `d ‚â• 1`

  - ‚Üí `/ 0` is impossible
  - ‚Üí `x / d <= x`

.

    atMost7
        |> N.divideBy n3
        --: N (In (Up0 minX_) (Up7 maxX_))
        |> N.toInt
values.type: N.N (N.In (N.Fixed (N.Add1 divisorMinMinus1_)) divisorMax_) -> N.N (N.In min_ max) -> N.N (N.In (N.Up0 minX_) max)

values.name: exactly
values.comment:
Create an [`N`](#N) with the value = minimum = maximum of a given difference

    N.inInt ( n3, n6 ) 5
        |> N.min
        |> N.exactly
        |> N.max
        |> N.differenceToInt
    --> 3

See [`differenceToInt`](#differenceToInt)
values.type: N.Up low N.To high -> N.N (N.In (N.Up low N.To high) (N.Up low N.To high))

values.name: fixedFromValue
values.comment: [`Fixed`](#Fixed) ‚Üí equatable [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)
values.type: N.FixedValue n -> N.Fixed n

values.name: fixedToValue
values.comment: [`Fixed`](#Fixed) ‚Üí equatable [`FixedValue`](#FixedValue)
values.type: N.Fixed n -> N.FixedValue n

values.name: fromValue
values.comment:
Number with equatable [`FixedValue` range](#InFixedValue)
‚Üí number with [`Fixed` range](#InFixed) to be [altered](#alter), [compared](#compare), ...
values.type: N.N (N.InFixedValue min max) -> N.N (N.InFixed min max)

values.name: greater
values.comment:
The maximum of both given numbers in the same range

Even though you can just use directly

    N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 3
        |> N.greater (N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 1)
        |> N.toInt
    --> 3

    N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 3
        |> N.greater (N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 4)
        |> N.toInt
    --> 4

this is rather supposed to be used as a primitive to build a structure maximum function:

    ArraySized.fold Up N.greater

For clamping, try [`atLeast`](#atLeast) instead!
values.type: N.N range -> N.N range -> N.N range

values.name: inFixedFromValue
values.comment: equatable [`InFixedValue`](#InFixedValue) ‚Üí [`InFixed`](#InFixed)
values.type: N.InFixedValue min max -> N.InFixed min max

values.name: inFixedToValue
values.comment: [`InFixed`](#InFixed) ‚Üí equatable [`InFixedValue`](#InFixedValue)
values.type: N.InFixed min max -> N.InFixedValue min max

values.name: inFuzz
values.comment:
`Fuzzer` for an [`N`](#N) in a given range.
For larger ranges, smaller [`N`](#N)s are preferred

    import Fuzz

    N.inFuzz ( n3, n6 )
        |> Fuzz.map N.toInt
        |> Fuzz.examples 10
    --> [ 5, 6, 3, 3, 4, 6, 3, 6, 3, 5 ]
values.type: ( N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin (N.Up lowerLimitMaxToUpperLimitMin_ N.To upperLimitMin)), N.N (N.In (N.Fixed upperLimitMin) upperLimitMax) ) -> Fuzz.Fuzzer (N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin upperLimitMax))

values.name: inFuzzUniform
values.comment:
`Fuzzer` for an [`N`](#N) in a given range.
In contrast to [`N.inFuzz`](#inFuzz),
all [`N`](#N)s are equally as likely, even for larger ranges

    import Fuzz

    N.inFuzzUniform ( n2, n2 |> N.toPower n13 )
        |> Fuzz.map N.toInt
        |> Fuzz.examples 3
    --> [ 2244, 4842, 3566 ]

    N.inFuzz ( n2, n2 |> N.toPower n13 )
        |> Fuzz.map N.toInt
        |> Fuzz.examples 3
    --> [ 26, 82, 17 ]
values.type: ( N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin (N.Up lowerLimitMaxToUpperLimitMin_ N.To upperLimitMin)), N.N (N.In (N.Fixed upperLimitMin) upperLimitMax) ) -> Fuzz.Fuzzer (N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin upperLimitMax))

values.name: inInt
values.comment:
Create a `N (In ...)` by **clamping** an `Int` between a minimum & maximum

  - if the `Int < minimum`, `minimum` is returned
  - if the `Int > maximum`, `maximum` is returned

If you want to handle the cases `< minimum` & `> maximum` explicitly, use [`isInInt`](#isInInt)

    0
        |> N.inInt ( n3, n12 )
        --: N (In (Up3 minX_) (Up12 minX_))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 3

    99
        |> N.inInt ( n3, n12 )
        |> N.toInt
    --> 12

    9
        |> N.inInt ( n3, n12 )
        |> N.toInt
    --> 9


    toDigit : Char -> Maybe (N (In (Up0 minX_) (Up9 maxX_)))
    toDigit char =
        ((char |> Char.toCode) - ('0' |> Char.toCode))
            |> N.isInInt ( n0, n9 )
            |> Result.toMaybe
values.type: ( N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin (N.Up lowerLimitMaxToUpperLimitMin_ N.To upperLimitMin)), N.N (N.In (N.Fixed upperLimitMin) upperLimitMax) ) -> Basics.Int -> N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin upperLimitMax)

values.name: inRandom
values.comment:
Generate a random [`N`](#N) in a range

    N.inRandom ( n1, n10 )
    --: Random.Generator
    --:     (N (In (Up1 minX_) (Up10 maxX_)))
values.type: ( N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin (N.Up lowerLimitMaxToUpperLimitMin_ N.To upperLimitMin)), N.N (N.In (N.Fixed upperLimitMin) upperLimitMax) ) -> Random.Generator (N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin upperLimitMax))

values.name: in_
values.comment:
**Clamp** the number to between both given limits

    between5And9 |> N.in_ ( n10, n10 )
    --: N (In (Up10 minX_) (Up10 maxX_))

    between5And15 |> N.in_ ( n5, n10 )
    --: N (In (Up5 minX_) (Up10 maxX_))

    atLeast5 |> N.in_ ( n5, n10 )
    --: N (In (Up5 minX_) (Up10 maxX_))

  - There shouldn't be an upper limit? ‚Üí [`atLeastMin`](#atLeastMin)
  - Keep the current maximum ‚Üí [`atLeast`](#atLeast)
  - Keep the current minimum ‚Üí [`atMost`](#atMost)

(The type doesn't forbid that the limits you're comparing against
are beyond the current limits)
values.type: ( N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin (N.Up minNewMaxToMaxNewMin_ N.To upperLimitMin)), N.N (N.In (N.Fixed upperLimitMin) upperLimitMax) ) -> N.N range_ -> N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin upperLimitMax)

values.name: is
values.comment:
Is the [`N`](#N) equal to, [`BelowOrAbove`](#BelowOrAbove) a given number?

    giveAPresent { age } =
        case age |> N.is n18 of
            Err (N.Below younger) ->
                toy { age = younger }

            Err (N.Above older) ->
                book { age = older }

            Ok _ ->
                bigPresent

    toy : { age : N (In min_ (Up17 maxX_)) } -> Toy

    book :
        { age : N (In (Fixed (Add19 minMinus19_)) max_) }
        -> Book

(The type doesn't forbid that the number you're comparing against
is below the current minimum or above the current maximum.
‚Üí `Err` or `Ok` values don't necessarily follow `min <= max` for `N (In min max ...)`
Luckily that's not a problem, since the values won't be produced anyway.)
values.type: N.N (N.In (N.Up minX N.To (N.Add1 comparedAgainstMinPlusXMinus1)) (N.Up maxX N.To (N.Add1 comparedAgainstMaxPlusXMinus1))) -> N.N (N.In min max) -> Result.Result (N.BelowOrAbove (N.N (N.In min (N.Up maxX N.To comparedAgainstMaxPlusXMinus1))) (N.N (N.In (N.Up minX N.To (N.Add2 comparedAgainstMinPlusXMinus1)) max))) (N.N (N.In (N.Up minX N.To (N.Add1 comparedAgainstMinPlusXMinus1)) (N.Up maxX N.To (N.Add1 comparedAgainstMaxPlusXMinus1))))

values.name: isAtLeast
values.comment:
Is the [`N`](#N) below than or at least as big as a given number?

    vote :
        { age : N (In (Fixed (Add18 minMinus18_)) max_) }
        -> Vote

    tryToVote { age } =
        case age |> N.isAtLeast n18 of
            Err _ ->
                -- üòì
                Nothing

            Ok oldEnough ->
                vote { age = oldEnough } |> Just

    factorial : N (In min_ max_) -> N (Min (Up1 x_))
    factorial =
        factorialBody

    factorialBody : N (In min_ max_) -> N (Min (Up1 x_))
    factorialBody x =
        case x |> N.isAtLeast n1 of
            Err _ ->
                n1 |> N.maxToInfinity

            Ok atLeast1 ->
                factorial (atLeast1 |> N.subtractMin n1)
                    |> N.multiplyBy atLeast1

(The type doesn't forbid that the lower limit you're comparing against
is below the current minimum or above the current maximum.
‚Üí `Err` or `Ok` values don't necessarily follow `min <= max` for `N (In min max ...)`
Luckily that's not a problem, since the values won't be produced anyway.)
values.type: N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin (N.Up lowerLimitMaxX N.To (N.Add1 lowerLimitMaxMinus1PlusX))) -> N.N (N.In min max) -> Result.Result (N.N (N.In min (N.Up lowerLimitMaxX N.To lowerLimitMaxMinus1PlusX))) (N.N (N.In lowerLimitMin max))

values.name: isAtLeastInt
values.comment:
If the `Int ‚â•` a given `minimum`,
return `Ok` with the `N (Min minimum)`,
else `Err` with the input `Int`

    4 |> N.isAtLeastInt n5
    --: Result Int (N (Min (Up5 x_)))
    --> Err 4

    1234 |> N.isAtLeastInt n5 |> Result.map N.toInt
    --> Ok 1234
values.type: N.N (N.In min max_) -> Basics.Int -> Result.Result Basics.Int (N.N (N.Min min))

values.name: isAtMost
values.comment:
Is the [`N`](#N) at most (`Ok`) or greater than (`Err`) a given number?

    goToBelow18Party : { age : N (In min_ (Up maxTo17 To N17)) } -> Snack

    tryToGoToBelow18Party { age } =
        case age |> N.isAtMost n17 of
            Ok below18 ->
                goToBelow18Party { age = below18 } |> Just

            Err _ ->
                Nothing

(The type doesn't forbid that the upper limit you're comparing against
is below the current minimum or above the current maximum.
‚Üí `Err` or `Ok` values don't necessarily follow `min <= max` for `N (In min max ...)`
Luckily that's not a problem, since the values won't be produced anyway.)
values.type: N.N (N.In (N.Up upperLimitMinX N.To upperLimitMinPlusX) upperLimitMax) -> N.N (N.In min max) -> Result.Result (N.N (N.In (N.Up upperLimitMinX N.To (N.Add1 upperLimitMinPlusX)) max)) (N.N (N.In min upperLimitMax))

values.name: isIn
values.comment:
Compared to a range from a lower to an upper bound,
is the [`N`](#N) in range or [`BelowOrAbove`](#BelowOrAbove)?

    isIn3To10 : N (In min_ max_) -> Maybe (N (In (Up3 minX_) (Up10 maxX_)))
    isIn3To10 =
        N.isIn ( n3, n10 ) >> Result.toMaybe

    n9 |> isIn3To10 |> Maybe.map N.toInt
    --> Just 9

    n12 |> isIn3To10
    --> Nothing

(The type doesn't forbid that the limits you're comparing against
are below the current minimum, above the current maximum or in the wrong order.
‚Üí `Err` or `Ok` values don't necessarily follow `min <= max` for `N (In min max ...)`
Luckily that's not a problem, since the values won't be produced anyway.)

Here's some example-cases:

  - `hi < lo < min < max` ‚Üí `Below | Above`
  - `hi < min < lo < max` ‚Üí `Below | Above`
  - `hi < min < max < lo` ‚Üí `Below | Above`
  - `lo < hi < min < max` ‚Üí `Above`
  - `max < hi < min < lo` ‚Üí `Below`
  - `min < hi < lo < max` ‚Üí `Below | Above`
  - `lo < min < hi < max` ‚Üí `Ok | Above`
  - `max < min < hi < lo` ‚Üí `Below`
  - `max < lo < hi < min` ‚Üí `Below | Ok | Above` ‚Üê the default case
  - `lo < min < max < hi` ‚Üí `Ok`
  - `min < lo < max < hi` ‚Üí `Ok | Below`
  - `min < max < lo < hi` ‚Üí `Below`

.

  - did we miss something? ‚Üí issue
  - feel motivated to add the other cases? ‚Üí PR

values.name: isInInt
values.comment:
Compared to a range from a lower to an upper bound, is the `Int` in range, [`BelowOrAbove`](#BelowOrAbove)?

    inputIntJudge : Int -> Result String (N (In (Up1 minX_) (Up10 maxX_)))
    inputIntJudge =
        N.isInInt ( n1, n10 )
            >> Result.mapError
                (\outOfRange ->
                    case outOfRange of
                        N.Below _ ->
                            "‚â§ 0"
                        N.Above _ ->
                            "‚â• 11"
                )

    0 |> inputIntJudge
    --> Err "‚â§ 0"

values.name: maxTo
values.comment:
Make it fit into functions with require a higher maximum

You should type arguments and stored types as broad as possible

    onlyAtMost18 : N (In min_ (Up maxTo18_ To N18)) -> ...

    onlyAtMost18 between3And8 -- works

But once you implement `onlyAtMost18`, you might use the `n` in `onlyAtMost19`:

    onlyAtMost18 n =
        -- onlyAtMost19 n ‚Üí error
        onlyAtMost19 (n |> N.maxTo n18)

values.name: maxToInfinity
values.comment:

allow `max` to go [up to infinity](#Infinity) to get a `N (Min min)`

    --: N (Min (Up3 x_))

Use it to unify different types of number minimum constraints like

    [ atLeast1, between1And10 ]

elm complains:

> But all the previous elements in the list are: `N (Min (Up1 x_))`

    [ atLeast1
    , between1And10 |> N.maxToInfinity
    ]

values.name: modByInt
values.comment:
Perform [modular arithmetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Modular_arithmetic) by an [`N`](#N) `d ‚â• 1`.
We know `x % d ‚â§ d - 1`

    7
        |> N.modByInt n3
        --: N (In (Up0 minX_) (Up2 maxX_))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 1

Works in the typical mathematical way for a negative `Int`

    -7
        |> N.modByInt n3
        --: N (In (Up0 minX_) (Up2 maxX_))
        |> N.toInt
    --> 2

Already have an [`N`](#N)? ‚Üí [`remainderBy`](#remainderBy)

values.name: smaller
values.comment:
The minimum of both given numbers in the same range

Even though you can just use directly

    N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 3
        |> N.smaller (N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 1)
        |> N.toInt
    --> 1

    N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 3
        |> N.smaller (N.inInt ( n0, n10 ) 4)
        |> N.toInt
    --> 3

this is rather supposed to be used as a primitive to build a structure maximum function:

    ArraySized.fold Up N.smaller

For clamping, try [`atMost`](#atMost) instead!

        |> N.toValue
    --> n1 |> N.toValue
